claim: The YOLO evidence dispatch boundary snapshot system combines git ls-files with recursive file walks, excludes only allowed artifact roots, and detects mutations across both git-backed and non-git projects.

evidence.summary: gitFiles() uses git ls-files -co --exclude-standard -z to capture tracked, cached, and other files.
evidence.why: This is the primary file discovery mechanism for git-backed projects.

evidence.summary: walkFiles() recursively walks directories excluding .git, node_modules, dist, coverage, .next, .cache.
evidence.why: This is the fallback mechanism for non-git projects or to catch untracked files.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot() merges git and walk results, excludes allowed roots, and creates SHA256 digests.
evidence.why: This is the core snapshot function that combines both file discovery strategies.

evidence.summary: Snapshot merges git files with walk files using Set union: [...new Set([...git, ...walkFiles()])]
evidence.why: This ensures both git-tracked and untracked files are included in the snapshot.

evidence.summary: diffBoundarySnapshots() compares before/after snapshots and reports added, modified, and deleted changes.
evidence.why: This is the mutation detection logic that catches project file changes.

evidence.summary: Runtime builds boundary before agents run, passes output_dir as allowed root, and builds again after execution.
evidence.why: This shows the actual boundary enforcement in action during evidence dispatch.

evidence.summary: After execution, boundary diff changes are converted to BOUNDARY_PROJECT_MUTATION blockers if any project files changed.
evidence.why: This is the enforcement logic that blocks PRD readiness when mutations are detected.

evidence.summary: Test git-backed boundary detects untracked project mutations created by evidence agents.
evidence.why: This proves the system catches new files not tracked by git.

evidence.summary: Test git-backed boundary detects gitignored project mutations outside excluded dirs.
evidence.why: This proves the system catches .env and other gitignored files that mutations could create.

evidence.summary: Test non-git boundary fallback detects modified project files and ignores artifacts.
evidence.why: This proves the walk-based fallback works for non-git projects and correctly excludes artifact roots.

evidence.summary: Test agents spawn writeFileSync() to create mutations during dispatch, then boundary blocks them.
evidence.why: This shows the realistic scenario of an agent actually modifying the project and being caught.

evidence.summary: Assertion verifies artifact writes are excluded: changes.some((c) => c.path.includes(agent-scratch.txt)) === false
evidence.why: This proves the allowed artifact root exclusion works correctly.

evidence.summary: excludedDir() filters .git, node_modules, dist, coverage, .next, .cache from both git and walk scans.
evidence.why: This defines the directories excluded from boundary snapshots.

assumptions: The git ls-files -co flag combination captures tracked, cached, and other files as documented by git.
SHA256 file digests are sufficient for detecting content changes.
The evidence dispatch artifact root is the only directory that should allow writes during evidence gathering.
risks: If git ls-files behavior changes in future git versions, the snapshot may miss files.
If an agent creates a mutation inside excluded directories like node_modules, it would not be detected.
If an agent creates a file inside the allowed artifact root that then influences project state, the boundary check would not catch it.

result.notes: The boundary snapshot implementation is verified to combine git ls-files with recursive walks, exclude allowed roots, and detect mutations. All key test cases pass including untracked, gitignored, and non-git scenarios.
